problem_summary: The 2024 MCM Problem C asked teams to analyze momentum in tennis matches: develop a metric for player performance/momentum over time, determine whether momentum swings are random, predict turning points, identify contributing factors, and generalize the model to other matches using provided Wimbledon data.
models_used: White noise tests (ACF, PACF, Ljung-Box, Box-Pierce)
key_techniques: Construction of a 5-dimension, 18-indicator hierarchical performance assessment system
Data cleaning: accumulation of binary variables, missing value imputation, categorical mapping
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for objective dimensionality reduction and weight derivation
TOPSIS for comprehensive scoring based on distance to ideal solutions
KMO and Bartlett's sphericity tests for model validation
White noise tests (ACF, PACF, Ljung-Box, Box-Pierce) to assess randomness of momentum sequences
GM(1,1) grey model on catastrophic sequences for small-sample disaster (turning point) prediction
Grey relational analysis to quantify factor impact on momentum swings
Cross-match validation (men's semifinal, women's final) to test generalizability
Sensitivity analysis by perturbing the weight of the most influential indicator
why_it_won: The paper comprehensively addresses all problem tasks with a well-integrated modeling pipeline: it creates a robust, objectively weighted PCA-TOPSIS momentum metric, statistically refutes randomness via multiple white noise tests, delivers accurate small-sample turning-point predictions using grey theory, and provides actionable advice backed by grey relational analysis. Rigorous validation (KMO, Bartlett's, sensitivity analysis, and cross-match testing) combined with clear visualizations and a strongly structured narrative made it an O‑prize work.
section_highlights: The writing excels through a crystal-clear 'Overview of Our Work' flowchart, well-organized sections that mirror the problem tasks, and a step‑by‑step exposition of each model's purpose, mathematical construction, and diagnostic outcomes. Tables, heatmaps, autocorrelation plots, and prediction-vs‑actual line graphs are used effectively to support the narrative without clutter.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Indicator set is not exhaustive; omits health, court environment, and psychological factors.
GM(1,1) model is only suitable for short‑to‑medium‑term prediction; not robust for long‑term forecasting.
Applying the model to different match types (e.g., women's) requires parameter and indicator adjustments.
Predictions in the women's final showed slightly lower accuracy compared to men's matches.
The serve‑count indicator contributed negligibly to the PCA model, hinting that some features may be redundant.
reusable_patterns: Build a domain-specific hierarchical indicator system with clear topical dimensions.
Combine PCA for objective weight determination and TOPSIS for composite scoring of time-series data.
Use statistical randomness tests (ACF/PACF, Ljung-Box) to validate or refute hypotheses about sequence behavior.
Apply GM(1,1) grey prediction on extracted anomaly/turning-point subsequences for small-sample forecasting.
Quantify influential factors via grey relational analysis and translate correlations into actionable strategic advice.
Always test model generalizability on held‑out or different-type matches and perform sensitivity analysis on key parameters.
Present results with stacked visual comparisons (predicted vs. actual turning points) and diagnostic metric tables.
